data.title: Manufacturing Down for 9th Month in November
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

U.S. manufacturing output shrank for the ninth month in a row in November. This long stretch of decline came after a short two-month period of growth, which itself followed 26 months of decreasing. Even though factories kept shrinking, the U.S. economy as a whole kept growing for the 67th month in a row.

The latest report from the Institute for Supply Management (ISM) indicates that while production expanded slightly, key indexes such as New Orders and Employment contracted at a faster rate, underscoring ongoing economic uncertainty.

Susan Spence, Chair of the ISM Manufacturing Business Survey Committee, reported that the Manufacturing PMI registered 48.2% in November. This figure represents a 0.5-percentage-point decrease from the 48.7% recorded in October. A reading below 50% signifies contraction in the manufacturing sector.

Spence noted that “In November, U.S. manufacturing activity contracted at a faster rate, with pullbacks in supplier deliveries, new orders and employment leading to the 0.5-percentage point decrease of the Manufacturing PMI.”

Key report’s highlights:

  • The Manufacturing PMI registered 48.2%, indicating contraction for the ninth consecutive month, and at a faster rate than in October.
  • The New Orders Index contracted for the third consecutive month, registering 47.4% (a 2.0-percentage point decrease).
  • The Production Index returned to expansion, registering 51.4%, a substantial increase of 3.2 percentage points from October’s figure.
  • The Employment Index contracted at a faster rate, registering 44.0%, marking its 10th consecutive month of decline.
  • The Supplier Deliveries Index indicated faster delivery performance for the first time in four months, registering 49.3%.
  • The Inventories Index contracted at a slower rate, registering 48.9%.
  • The Customers’ Inventories Index registered 44.7%, remaining firmly in ‘too low’ territory for the 14th straight month, a status usually considered positive for future production.
  • The Prices Index remained in expansion (increasing) for the 14th straight month, registering 58.5%, a slightly faster rate than in October.
  • The Backlog of Orders Index contracted at a faster rate, registering 44.0%, indicating order backlogs contracted for the 38th consecutive month.
  • The New Export Orders Index contracted for the ninth consecutive month, registering 46.2%.
  • The Imports Index remained in contraction for the eighth consecutive month, registering 48.9%.

Demand, output, and employment show volatility

Measures of demand, especially the New Orders Index, shrank for the third month in a row, coming in at 47.4%. Spence said that drops in two of the four demand measures (Backlog of Orders and New Orders) canceled out the gains from New Export Orders and Customers’ Inventories. The Backlog of Orders Index shrank further to 44%, marking the 38th consecutive month of decline.

For output, the Production Index rose in November to 51.4%. This good news followed a recent pattern where improvement in one area leads to gains in another, according to Spence. Of the six biggest manufacturing sectors, three made more goods in November: Food, Beverage & Tobacco Products; Computer & Electronic Products; and Machinery.

The Employment Index was 44%, shrinking even faster for the 10th month in a row. Companies kept cutting jobs because they are unsure about future demand. Spence pointed out that most companies are still managing staff numbers, with 67% of panelists saying this is their approach, the same as in October. Also, for every comment about hiring, there were 3.4 about cutting jobs.

Trade friction and costs influence supply chain

Inputs, which include supplier deliveries, inventories, prices, and imports, showed mixed results in November. The Supplier Deliveries Index was 49.3%, indicating deliveries accelerated after three months of delays. The Inventories Index shrank more slowly, rising to 48.9%.

The Prices Index remained in expansion (indicating raw material prices increased) for the 14th straight month, registering 58.5%. Spence noted that “The Prices Index reading continues to be driven by increases in steel and aluminum prices that impact the entire value chain, as well as tariffs applied to many imported goods.”

Export and import orders kept shrinking, but not as quickly as before. The New Export Orders Index shrank for the ninth month in a row, coming in at 46.2%. Spence said that even though the index improved by 1.7 points, trade problems are still hurting demand, and many panelists still see weaker international orders because of tariffs and ongoing uncertainty about U.S. economic policy. The Imports Index kept shrinking for the eighth month, coming in at 48.9 percent, which Spence said shows that tariffs and weaker demand are still affecting buying decisions.

Outlook remains challenging as backlog contracts

The report showed that trade policy and global uncertainty are having a big impact on business. While production grew for a short time, the Manufacturing PMI shrank even faster overall. In November, 58% of the sector’s total output decreased. But the share of output shrinking the most (with a PMI of 45% or less) fell slightly, to 39% from 41% in October.

Spence emphasized that “Another month of contraction in the Backlog of Orders Index suggests that trade-related and geopolitical factors persist. Not much improvement is expected until those influences diminish.”

​

data.title: IQM Invests €40M to Expand Quantum Chip Production Facility in Finland
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

IQM Quantum Computers announced it will invest more than €40 million to expand its production site in Espoo, Finland, and build up to 30 full-stack quantum computers per year. The company said it expects the project to be completed in the first quarter of 2026. Once completed, the site will span 8,000 square meters, nearly doubling both cleanroom space and system assembly line capacity.

The investment follows IQM’s Series B fundraise of more than $320 million (~€275 million) and is intended to strengthen the company’s roadmap toward fault-tolerant quantum computing by 2030. IQM’s long-term target, stated in its roadmap unveiled in late 2024, is to achieve “1 million qubits quantum computers by 2033,” IQM’s co-CEO and co-founder Jan Goetz told EE Times Europe.

Pasi Kivinen, IQM’s VP of operations, described the expanded site as “one of the world’s most advanced production facilities for quantum computers, combining assembly lines and chip production.” He said the integration will “enable us to scale up in critical areas which are essential to deliver quality, quantity, and stable, advanced solutions to the market.”

Goetz told EE Times Europe that the designation is based on three criteria: “Shorter lead time to produce the system to the end-user—faster (four months) than competitors,” the integration of “the quantum chip fabrication and system assembly line … under the same facility, together with the chip design teams,” and the fact that “this production facility is dedicated to quantum computing.”

The Espoo site will also adopt environmental measures, including an abatement system for direct emissions and a move to fully renewable district heating.

Toward fault tolerance

Goetz told EE Times Europe that IQM finds itself in the so-called noisy intermediate-scale quantum era, the current phase in quantum computing. “Our focus is on building and improving quantum computers with a number of qubits while working to enhance their performance and reduce errors,” he said. IQM has delivered systems with 5, 20, and 50 qubits and aims to reach 150 qubits by 2026.

The next stage of the roadmap is the creation of quantum error-correction demonstrators. This includes developing logical qubits using multiple physical qubits, in which multiple physical qubits are used to encode a single, more stable, and reliable qubit. The goal for this stage is to build systems with logical qubits that have significantly lower error rates. “To achieve this, we are working on a novel chip topology that merges our ‘Star’ and ‘Crystal’ designs, which we believe will be more hardware-efficient than other approaches,” Goetz said. 

The company plans to reach fault-tolerant systems by 2030 through a modular architecture designed to support hundreds or thousands of logical qubits with error rates low enough to perform complex computations without being corrupted by noise.

Future capacity and global footprint

IQM acknowledges that additional manufacturing capacity will be required beyond the current expansion. Goetz said, “In addition to our current efforts to expand our capabilities, we envision further investments into advanced quantum chip production in the future. IQM is committed to enabling large-scale quantum computers. We will do this in our own production facilities and together with partners.”

Asked why the company is not expanding at a faster pace, given the size of the Series B financing, Goetz said, “At the moment, we’re investing €40 million, and we will announce our plans for further investment in the future.”

The company said it is hiring to support growth plans. “We are actively hiring the best talent all the time from the global market and are attracting international talent to Europe,” Goetz said.

In September, IQM had sold 15 systems, with more than 10 quantum computers delivered worldwide. “Our customers include universities, HPC centers, and research institutions,” Goetz said. “The demand varies from customer to customer, and we have standardized products with unique features, such as open architecture, that meet the needs of our customers globally.”

The company is expanding its global reach. It sees opportunities in Japan and India, where it expects to “collaborate with ecosystem players” and leverage government-backed programs. In Europe, its collaboration with CEA-Leti in Grenoble continues on next-generation chip and packaging technologies. IQM has also committed to an industrial-scale facility in France by 2027 as part of a €100 million project. 

When asked whether the 2027 objective remains in place, Goetz replied, “Our objectives are stated in our technology roadmap. To enable the roadmap, we will systematically invest in R&D, testing, and fabrication facilities to boost technology scaling up to 1 million qubits, while maintaining high qubit quality and gate fidelity. We are continuing our R&D efforts with partners such as CEA in France.”

European context

IQM’s expansion comes as Europe prepares new policy measures intended to strengthen quantum research capabilities and industrial capacity. In an opinion piece for EE Times Europe, co-founder and chief global affairs officer Juha Vartiainen recently wrote that the European Commission’s quantum strategy offers “a welcome and necessary blueprint that speaks of ambition, sovereignty, and a clear intent to position Europe as a global leader in the quantum age by 2030.” He noted that the strategy centers on research and innovation, infrastructure, ecosystem development, space and dual-use applications, and skills. The challenge, he wrote, will be execution, stressing that it presents “a rare opportunity to rethink our approach and push the boundaries of what’s possible—a mindset deeply ingrained in the quantum sector.”

Alongside the Quantum Strategy, Europe is preparing the EU Quantum Act, scheduled for adoption in 2026. The Act is expected to focus on three areas: boosting research and innovation, scaling industrial capacity through pilot lines and a design facility, and reinforcing supply-chain resilience and governance. The Quantum Strategy also includes a proposed Quantum Grand Challenge, which aims to support industrial-scale quantum computing platforms. Vartiainen described the mechanism as intended to “accelerate quantum innovation by supporting individual startups and scaleups” through a competitive and collaborative process. He wrote that by bringing together companies, researchers, and users, it could “significantly accelerate progress in quantum technologies.”

IQM sees this policy framework as relevant to the company’s growth plans. Goetz said, “We are looking forward to the EU Quantum Act and many important initiatives, including the Quantum Grand Challenge,” Goetz said these initiatives align with IQM’s efforts to strengthen the European ecosystem with the delivery of “next-generation systems to customers, including VTT in Finland, the Leibniz Supercomputing Center in Germany, and additional projects in Italy, Poland, Spain, and more.”

data.title: What Startups Need from Their Backers
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Europe’s science and technology landscape is vibrant. From the quantum labs of Delft to the AI hubs of Edinburgh, we continue to generate ideas that could reshape industries. But ideas alone don’t build companies. What transforms a breakthrough into a business is the quality of its support, especially from investors.

As someone who’s spent a career bridging science and finance, I’ve seen how fragile the early stages of building a company can be. The right investor can be the difference between a startup that scales and one that stalls. And in deep tech, where timelines are long and risks are real, that difference is magnified.

So what should investors bring to the table? Beyond capital, they must offer strategic patience, informed collaboration, and a commitment to long-term value creation.

Strategic patience: understanding the arc of innovation

Deep-tech ventures don’t follow the same trajectory as consumer apps or sales-as-a-service platforms. Their development cycles are longer, their regulatory paths more complex, and their market entry often delayed by technical validation. Investors who expect rapid returns will either distort the company’s direction or disengage too soon.

Instead, we need backers who understand that progress in these sectors is incremental but profound. The most transformative technologies, whether in semiconductors, synthetic biology, or AI infrastructure, require time to mature. Investors must be prepared to support that journey, not rush it.

This isn’t about being passive; it’s about being aligned. The best investors in deep tech are those who recognize that the real value lies in defensible IP, robust engineering, and scalable platforms. Chasing short-term revenue comes at the expense of long-term potential.

Informed collaboration: more than just money

While capital is essential, it’s not sufficient. Founders need investors who can help them navigate complex ecosystems by connecting them to industrial partners, regulatory experts, and international markets.

In deep tech, the investor’s role is often closer to that of a co-pilot than a financier. Investors must understand the science well enough to ask the right questions and the market well enough to guide commercialization. They should help founders stay focused on their core innovation while also preparing them for the realities of scaling.

Europe has a wealth of technical talent, but we are still short on investors with domain expertise in frontier technologies. That must change. We need more physicists, engineers, and policy thinkers in the investment community—people who can bridge the gap between the lab and the ledger.

Long-term value: building companies that stay

Europe’s challenge isn’t starting companies but keeping them. Too many promising ventures are acquired early, often by foreign buyers, before they reach full maturity. The result is a loss of strategic control, economic value, and technological sovereignty.

If the platforms that underpin our financial systems, energy grids, and healthcare diagnostics are built abroad, then we lose the ability to shape them according to our values and priorities.

To change this, we must strengthen the funding ladder, especially at the later stages. In the U.K., just 0.007% of pension assets are allocated to venture capital. That’s less than £1 in every £14,000. In North America, by contrast, the figure is up to 2%.

This is a missed opportunity. Britain has one of the largest savings industries in the world, with pension assets per capita far above the global average. Our savers are long-term investors, yet their capital is locked into short-term instruments. We must unlock it.

If pension trustees allocated just 0.5% of assets to venture capital, it would represent a seventy-fold increase—still within prudent risk thresholds but transformative for our innovation economy.

Advice to founders: choose your allies wisely

To founders, my message is clear: Not all capital is created equal. Look for investors who understand your technology, respect your mission, and are prepared to support you through the inevitable ups and downs.

Ask about their track record in deep tech. Do they have experience navigating regulatory hurdles? Can they help you build partnerships with corporations or governments? Will they back you when timelines stretch and challenges mount?

And don’t be afraid to demand more than funding. Ask for introductions, strategic input, and help with hiring. The best investors are those who see themselves as part of your journey and not just your balance sheet.

A new compact for innovation

Europe has the talent, and it has the ideas. What it needs is a new compact between founders and funders built on trust, expertise, and shared ambition.

If we get this right, we won’t just build successful companies; we’ll build industries that reflect our values, strengthen our economies, and secure our future.

Let’s back our innovators with the kind of support that lasts, because when we invest wisely and not just generously, we all benefit.

data.title: Could an EU Chips Act 2.0 Bridge the Lab-to-Fab Gap?
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

When the European Chips Act came into force in September 2023, it was hailed as Europe’s most ambitious industrial policy in decades. Its promise was bold: Double Europe’s share of global semiconductor production to 20% by 2030 and restore technological sovereignty in a field long dominated by the U.S. and Asia.

EE Times Europe reached out to the European Commission (EC) to find out what may come next. According to a spokesperson, the Chips Act has already catalyzed more than €80 billion in manufacturing investments and committed 85% of the €3.3 billion budget under the Chips for Europe initiative. Those results, the EC spokesperson said, “have laid a strong foundation for consolidating Europe’s position in the global semiconductor market after two decades of decline.”

Calls to revise the Chips Act

Nevertheless, in its two years of existence, the legislation has galvanized its fair share of critics. In April 2025, the European Court of Auditors (ECA) took stock of the progress and issued a sobering verdict: While the Chips Act had given Europe’s semiconductor ambitions a much-needed boost, the auditors concluded it was “very unlikely to be sufficient to reach the overly ambitious Digital Decade target” of a 20% global market share. Their report pointed to vague milestones, sluggish uptake of first-of-a-kind manufacturing projects, and a lingering disconnect between Europe’s research strengths and its ability to turn innovation into production.

The ECA also found that the system for financing chip initiatives remains overly complex and poorly coordinated. The EC directly manages only about 10% of the €43 billion in public funding, with the rest scattered across national and private programs. The result, the auditors reported, is a patchwork of projects that are “fragmented and underfunded.” Developers trying to move from lab prototypes to factory floors face lengthy approval cycles and cumbersome procedures that slow progress and discourage participation, especially among smaller companies.

The calls for change have been gaining momentum. On Sept. 29, all 27 EU member states signed a joint declaration calling for a “revised and forward-looking Chips Act.” The declaration urges the EU to move quickly toward a “Chips Act 2.0” that would align national and EU funding, accelerate Important Projects of Common European Interest (IPCEI) approvals, and strengthen Europe’s semiconductor leadership.

The EC spokesperson told EE Times Europe that the commission had already begun evaluating the Chips Act as part of a planned first quarter of 2026 review that was built into the regulation when it was adopted. To solicit input from stakeholders ahead of that review, the commission issued a call for evidence in September 2025 that would be open for 12 weeks.

While the EC review has been planned from the start, the ECA’s critique and the member states’ call for reform have raised the stakes. By 2026, Brussels will have to decide whether to adjust course or risk falling further behind the U.S. and Asia in the global chip race. In other words, it will have to decide whether there will indeed be a Chips Act 2.0.

A smoother transition from research to manufacturing

Jean-René Lèquepeys, CTO of CEA-Leti, agrees with the ECA finding that one area of potential improvement lies in the interaction between the first two of the three pillars defined by the Chips Act. Under Pillar 1, CEA-Leti and other research and technology organizations (RTOs) develop technologies up to about technology readiness level (TRL) 5 or 6, while manufacturers supported under Pillar 2 focus on high-volume production of industry-ready technology. According to Lèquepeys, the link between the two—the lab-to-fab transition through which prototypes become products—must be strengthened.

Currently, companies must pay the full cost of accessing pilot-line infrastructure just to evaluate results emerging from publicly funded research. Lèquepeys argues that this creates a disincentive to experiment with early-stage technologies, especially those at TRL 4 to 6, where uncertainty and cost remain high. “We need to find a bridge between Pillar 1 and Pillar 2,” he told EE Times Europe in an exclusive interview. “Industry should work more closely with the pilot lines to evaluate and test technologies that aren’t yet mature, and they should receive support in doing so.”

Lèquepeys proposes a shared-risk model, in which industrial partners receive partial funding when collaborating with RTOs on pre-industrialization work. Large companies might cover a significant portion of expenses, while small and midsized businesses could receive higher subsidies to offset risk.

Such an arrangement would encourage industry to adopt emerging technologies earlier, helping Europe achieve its long-term goal of increasing semiconductor self-sufficiency, Lèquepeys contends. “The trigger for success,” he said, “is the efficient and fast transfer of technologies from lab to fab, so that new processes become new products and research translates directly into industrial value.”

Lèquepeys also recommends improved cooperation among Europe’s major research institutes—including CEA-Leti, Fraunhofer, imec, Tyndall, Silicon Austria Labs, and VTT—to avoid duplication and to align resources. While the first Chips Act encouraged national pilot lines such as France’s FAMES, Germany’s APECS, and Belgium’s NanoIC, coordination among them needs to be improved. Lèquepeys hopes the Chips Act 2.0 will formalize a pan-European network where equipment, expertise, and intellectual property are shared rather than duplicated—a “virtual European research institute” connecting labs to fabs.

While Lèquepeys believes a new Chips Act could play a role in encouraging coordination, he doesn’t think Europe’s research institutes should wait around in the meantime. In early 2025, with support from other RTOs, CEA-Leti launched the RESOLVE initiative, a Europe-wide lab-to-fab accelerator pooling pilot-line infrastructure and coordinating development across 15 technology tracks—from new memory architectures and 3D integration to AI accelerators and wide-bandgap power devices.

Simpler procedures for funding

Another big obstacle facing European semiconductor programs is the current Chips Act’s bureaucratic labyrinth. Lèquepeys posits that the process for securing approval and funding under the act’s framework is too slow and fragmented to keep pace with the speed of technological change. Each project must navigate complex state-aid and competition rules before any national funding can flow to industrial partners. France cannot simply fund STMicroelectronics, and Germany cannot directly fund Infineon. Instead, every initiative must go through Brussels for authorization, which can take years.

This system, designed to ensure fair competition among the EU’s 27 member states, has the unintended effect of slowing down cross-border cooperation and discouraging rapid project adjustments once market conditions shift. Lèquepeys pointed to recent examples of large-scale manufacturing projects being delayed or canceled because of geopolitical and economic changes. Those course corrections have had a significant impact on the strategies of semiconductor manufacturing companies.

Lèquepeys believes the solution lies in expanding and modernizing the IPCEI mechanism, which already allows multiple countries to co-fund strategic initiatives. A Chips Act 2.0 built on IPCEI principles could give national governments more autonomy to co-finance R&D and manufacturing projects simultaneously, while maintaining transparency and fairness at the European level. This, he said, would “cut through the administrative delays that prevent good ideas from becoming industrial reality.”

Speed, in his view, has become just as critical as funding. Semiconductor innovation now moves in cycles measured in months rather than years, and Europe’s administrative processes must match that tempo. Simplifying procedures would not only help Europe deploy funds faster but also give research organizations and industrial partners the agility to adapt as technologies and markets evolve.

“We know projects change,” Lèquepeys said. “What we need is a structure that’s flexible enough to let them do so.”

data.title: How Europe Navigates Geopolitics in Pursuit of Semiconductor Sovereignty
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

The global semiconductor industry is on track to reach $1 trillion in revenue by 2030. Acknowledging the strategic value of semiconductor technologies and aiming to reduce external dependencies, governments around the world have introduced “chips acts” and sizable incentive packages to support the return of chip design and manufacturing to their own regions.

In Europe, the quest for technological sovereignty has become a major investment focus. The €43 billion EU Chips Act is catalyzing national projects and multinational alliances to build continental capacity across chip design, manufacturing, and advanced packaging. For example, ESMC, a joint venture among TSMC, NXP Semiconductors, Infineon Technologies, and Robert Bosch, is pursuing construction of a €10 billion 300-mm wafer fab in Dresden, Germany; GlobalFoundries anticipates a €1.1 billion investment to increase production capacity at its Dresden site to over 1 million wafers per year by 2028; and Infineon is expanding its Dresden operations, investing €4.46 billion in a fab for discrete power devices and analog/mixed-signal ICs. Looking south, STMicroelectronics is investing €5 billion in a SiC chip fab in Catania, Sicily, with €2 billion in approved state funding, while Singapore’s Silicon Box is setting up a €3.2 billion chiplet fab in Novara, Italy.

Yet the European Court of Auditors recently concluded that “despite reasonable progress in implementation, the Chips Act is very unlikely to be enough to reach the very ambitious Digital Decade target of a 20% share in the global market value chain by revenue” and pleaded for a “revised and forward-looking Chips Act.”

The call for European semiconductor sovereignty takes on another dimension in light of geopolitical intricacies, particularly those arising from the U.S.–China technology decoupling. In this Special Report, we investigate how Europe fits in a world with distinct technology blocs and how European semiconductor companies can navigate the challenges and capitalize on opportunities in this shifting global economy.

This Special Report relies on interviews with CEA-Leti, DataDirect Networks, École Centrale de Lyon, ESMC, the European Center for Medium-Range Weather Forecast, the European Commission, GlobalFoundries, the Grésivaudan Valley Community, Jülich Supercomputing Center, the Max Planck Institute for Meteorology, the Niels Bohr Institute, Silicon Box, Silicon Saxony, TSMC, and Yole Group, as well as a panel discussion led by representatives from DDN, the EuroHPC Joint Undertaking, Eviden, Leonardo, and SiPearl. It also features a collection of contributions by technology and market research firm IDTechEx, IP law firm EIP, and market observer Christoph Gnadl.

Could an EU Chips Act 2.0 Bridge the Lab-to-Fab Gap?

By Pat Brans

When the European Chips Act came into force in September 2023, it was hailed as Europe’s most ambitious industrial policy in decades. Its promise was bold: Double Europe’s share of global semiconductor production to 20% by 2030 and restore technological sovereignty in a field long dominated by the U.S. and Asia.

Europe’s Chips Act Stumbles Over Its Own Rigidity

By Pat Brans

The pause of the STMicroelectronics–GlobalFoundries megafab in Crolles exposes a core weakness in Europe’s semiconductor policy: Once money is locked in, it can’t move with the market.

Why the World’s Tech Giants Are Betting on Europe

By Rebecca Pool

U.S. and Asian investments look set to transform Europe’s semiconductor sector—but with overseas cash driving growth, who will shape the future?

Intel’s Retreat Shifts Europe Semiconductor Reality

By Pat Brans

When Intel announced in 2022 that it would build a €30 billion megafab in Magdeburg, Germany, and a $4.6 billion (~€4.2 billion) semiconductor assembly and test plant near Wroclaw, Poland, European leaders celebrated. Intel’s investments symbolized the prospect of strategic autonomy in chips, thousands of jobs, and global relevance in advanced semiconductor manufacturing—advancing progress toward the goals of the EU Chips Act.

Denmark Opens 300-mm Wafer Fab to Strengthen Europe’s Sovereignty

By Rebecca Pool

With the POEM Technology Center, Denmark breaks into 300-mm wafer production, advancing Europe’s exit from U.S. and Chinese chip reliance.

Europe’s Semiconductor Resilience: The Gaps Behind the Policy Goals

By Christoph H. X. Gnadl

Europe’s investment in strengthening its semiconductor independence risks neglecting the operational fundamentals that define resilience.

Sovereign AI: The New Foundation of National Power

By Yu-Han Chang, IDTechEx

Across the world, governments are beginning to treat AI infrastructure as a strategic national asset, essential for maintaining technological sovereignty.

Can JUPITER Power Europe’s AI Ambition?

By Rebecca Pool

Europe’s new exascale supercomputer opens the door to homegrown frontier AI and large-scale simulations that could transform science and deliver tech sovereignty.

European Technological Sovereignty: Visionary or Illusory?

By Anne-Françoise Pelé

The rhetoric around European technological sovereignty and strategic autonomy has intensified, but how can these ambitions be turned into concrete action?

OpenAI’s Broadcom Pact Underscores Europe’s Strategic Chip Moment

By Andrew Thompson, EIP

OpenAI’s partnership with Broadcom reflects a broader industry shift toward custom silicon as a means of achieving performance, autonomy, and strategic advantage.

data.title: Lemurian Labs Raises $28 Million For AI Portability Software
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

AI software startup Lemurian Labs has pivoted away from AI hardware, having raised a $28-million series A to build its AI software stack. Lemurian’s hardware-aware software stack will address performant training and inference at the edge in on-premises installations and in the heterogeneous cloud, as well as enable code portability between different hardware without rewrites, Lemurian CEO Jay Dawani told EE Times.

“This is the only time in history we could truly say, it’s time to go and rethink the system, we need to change how we do software,” Dawani said. “I think the thing that actually surprised me and everyone else here was that this is more doable than we thought. The problem is tractable, the reason no-one’s gone this route is they were too hung up on path-dependent thinking; the scale we’re already at, it’s a bit scary if you want to throw it all in the bin.”

Performance gains from Lemurian’s stack could be between 2× and 30×, depending on the workload, its scale and the hardware involved. The biggest improvements will be seen for training workloads, since the scale they require means they are harder to optimize manually, Dawani said.

Kernel-free

Lemurian started out as a chip company with a logarithmic number scheme designed for efficient AI inference. However, Dawani told EE Times last year that “there are enough hardware companies out there,” and that the company pivoted to fixing AI infrastructure’s relatively immature, complex and hardware-specific software stack.

Lemurian wants to build a software stack spanning compiler layers and workload orchestration that enables complete portability between heterogeneous hardware, enabling deployment of the same code in the cloud, on-premises servers and at the edge.

“What we ended up building is basically a unified software stack for heterogeneous computing at scale,” Dawani said.

Dawani sees AI hardware becoming more heterogeneous as the industry embraces chiplets, 3D stacking, inference-specific chips (those optimized for throughput versus latency), and more specialization on models and workloads.

Part of this vision is getting rid of kernels, i.e. lower-level programs that manage access to the hardware directly.

“The number of kernels you’d have to write [for every hardware option] is in the order of 1026,” Dawani said. “There aren’t enough good engineers in the world who can write those kernels. Even if you brute-force it with LLMs, you’d need to understand the microarchitecture details and inline instructions for every vendor’s hardware, and they don’t necessarily want to surface that information. That creates a whole new set of challenges.”

GPU kernels are a faulty abstraction, Dawani said, because they are generally oriented around optimizing the compute pipeline rather than optimizing for memory bandwidth, a common bottleneck in state-of-the-art AI systems. Kernels also do not solve the portability problem, since by default they are hardware- and workload-specific.

“In fact, if you write a good kernel today, it makes your hardware look worse,” he said. “You’re exposing the latency of your system when the job is to hide the latency. If you push a compute pipeline to the limit, you’re making memory bandwidth and everything else look worse, because you can’t get data in and out fast enough.”

At-scale computing requires a new programming model with a new compiler and runtime, Dawani said. Lemurian’s stack has runtimes at the device, node, rack and cluster level. The company is building a fully parallel optimizing compiler that is hardware aware, but optimized for linear algebra at scale.

“Now what you’re doing is you are programming a system of many heterogeneous parts as if it were one chip instead of programming each chip in that system in isolation,” he said.

Studying an exhaustive list of kernels and operators, as well as data movement and scheduling techniques, allowed the team to distil everything into 27-core parallel computing primitives. These primitives preserve enough information to allow the company’s compiler to do a richer set of hardware-informed optimizations, keeping everything as parallel as possible from the outset.

“Kernels reduce the amount of bandwidth in a system, because that assumes a pull model, with signaling overhead from fetch, stop and resume, and so on,” Dawani said. “We’ve turned everything, even in the GPU realm, into a push model, so everything is now distributed dataflow, or similar.”

Lemurian wants to shape compute around data movement costs, not the other way around. Viewing a large cluster as though it were a single chip means orchestration is already baked into its stack, Dawani said.

“We have the hardware insights and awareness to adaptively tune our scheduling at runtime and we can deal with node failures and other things,” he said. “The coolest part is we’ve changed how you program—you don’t have to deal with kernels any more—things like NCCL [Nvidia collective communications library] and how you tune it for different fabrics and topologies is baked into the scheduling.”

This approach also allows Lemurian to scale to more heterogeneous devices (i.e., those that are not kernel-oriented). The idea is to create an abstraction that works for all types of hardware.

“If you’re running kernels, you don’t know when you’re about to get thermally throttled or you’re losing bandwidth—we do,” he said. “We can keep you in areas of the Pareto curve of throughput or bandwidth per dollar that no other system can, which changes the economics of compute from a customer perspective.”

Customer workloads

Getting rid of kernels has secondary benefits, including enabling customers to iterate faster on their rapidly evolving workloads, Dawani said.

“Many companies are exploring beyond transformer architectures, and they need the ability to scale,” he added. “They shouldn’t be dependent on whether somebody wrote an optimized kernel library for that.”

Cutting out kernel development will change the innovation cycle for AI companies, Dawani said.

“Customers want to be able to go from a local development environment on a laptop to training on a cluster, to then get that model into their customer’s hands faster,” he said. “Once a model is trained, there’s months with an army of kernel writers who will optimize the model for deployment. What if that six-month period could become two days?”

In terms of productization, Dawani said the company does not want to interfere with customers’ cloud buys, allowing them to set up a compute environment that suits them, while Lemurian’s stack will deploy as a control plane “to help them get the most out of that.”

“We primarily support PyTorch and our goal is to make PyTorch go to the limits of device physics,” he said, noting that customers who want more control than PyTorch can offer will be able to use a Lemurian domain-specific language to squeeze even more performance out of the same hardware.

Lemurian will also have a serving engine to help scale models in deployment and cater to different service-level agreements that may be required. A training engine will offer support for large homogeneous or heterogeneous clusters, or runs spread across more than one cluster.

Lemurian’s serving and inference stack is on track to launch at the end of next summer, Dawani said.

data.title: Lemurian Labs Raises $35 Million For AI Portability Software
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

AI software startup Lemurian Labs has pivoted away from AI hardware, having raised a $35-million series A to build its AI software stack. Lemurian’s hardware-aware software stack will address performant training and inference at the edge in on-premises installations and in the heterogeneous cloud, as well as enable code portability between different hardware without rewrites, Lemurian CEO Jay Dawani told EE Times.

“This is the only time in history we could truly say, it’s time to go and rethink the system, we need to change how we do software,” Dawani said. “I think the thing that actually surprised me and everyone else here was that this is more doable than we thought. The problem is tractable, the reason no-one’s gone this route is they were too hung up on path-dependent thinking; the scale we’re already at, it’s a bit scary if you want to throw it all in the bin.”

Performance gains from Lemurian’s stack could be between 2× and 30×, depending on the workload, its scale and the hardware involved. The biggest improvements will be seen for training workloads, since the scale they require means they are harder to optimize manually, Dawani said.

Kernel-free

Lemurian started out as a chip company with a logarithmic number scheme designed for efficient AI inference. However, Dawani told EE Times last year that “there are enough hardware companies out there,” and that the company pivoted to fixing AI infrastructure’s relatively immature, complex and hardware-specific software stack.

Lemurian wants to build a software stack spanning compiler layers and workload orchestration that enables complete portability between heterogeneous hardware, enabling deployment of the same code in the cloud, on-premises servers and at the edge.

“What we ended up building is basically a unified software stack for heterogeneous computing at scale,” Dawani said.

Dawani sees AI hardware becoming more heterogeneous as the industry embraces chiplets, 3D stacking, inference-specific chips (those optimized for throughput versus latency), and more specialization on models and workloads.

Part of this vision is getting rid of kernels, i.e. lower-level programs that manage access to the hardware directly.

“The number of kernels you’d have to write [for every hardware option] is in the order of 1026,” Dawani said. “There aren’t enough good engineers in the world who can write those kernels. Even if you brute-force it with LLMs, you’d need to understand the microarchitecture details and inline instructions for every vendor’s hardware, and they don’t necessarily want to surface that information. That creates a whole new set of challenges.”

GPU kernels are a faulty abstraction, Dawani said, because they are generally oriented around optimizing the compute pipeline rather than optimizing for memory bandwidth, a common bottleneck in state-of-the-art AI systems. Kernels also do not solve the portability problem, since by default they are hardware- and workload-specific.

“In fact, if you write a good kernel today, it makes your hardware look worse,” he said. “You’re exposing the latency of your system when the job is to hide the latency. If you push a compute pipeline to the limit, you’re making memory bandwidth and everything else look worse, because you can’t get data in and out fast enough.”

At-scale computing requires a new programming model with a new compiler and runtime, Dawani said. Lemurian’s stack has runtimes at the device, node, rack and cluster level. The company is building a fully parallel optimizing compiler that is hardware aware, but optimized for linear algebra at scale.

“Now what you’re doing is you are programming a system of many heterogeneous parts as if it were one chip instead of programming each chip in that system in isolation,” he said.

Studying an exhaustive list of kernels and operators, as well as data movement and scheduling techniques, allowed the team to distil everything into 27-core parallel computing primitives. These primitives preserve enough information to allow the company’s compiler to do a richer set of hardware-informed optimizations, keeping everything as parallel as possible from the outset.

data.title: Germany Redirects Intel Funds Into New Wave of Semiconductor Projects
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Germany has quietly launched one of its most consequential semiconductor funding efforts since the start of the EU Chips Act. Originally intended in part to support Intel’s long-delayed €30 billion (approximately $34.8 billion) megafab in Magdeburg, billions of euros in federal and state money are now being re-channeled into a diverse set of technology projects, ranging from mature-node capacity in Dresden to quantum-sensor-based metrology equipment in Munich.

The catalyst was a late-2024 federal call for projects to fall under the EU Chips Act, a mechanism Germany had never used in its semiconductor policy. Up to then, major undertakings—Infineon’s power semiconductor expansion, ESMC in Dresden, Wolfspeed in Saarland, and Intel itself—had emerged through direct negotiations rather than a competitive public call. For the first time, Germany opened a structured process, intended both to accelerate high-priority microelectronics investments and prevent semiconductor-earmarked money from being cannibalized for unrelated parts of the federal budget.

Frank Bösenberg, managing director of Silicon Saxony, believes Intel’s delays played a decisive role. “This was already after Intel did not appear to be full-speed,” he told EE Times in an exclusive interview. “It wasn’t yet canceled; it was just delayed. So, I guess the motivation was to implement something—and to have a fair, more or less transparent process for getting money.”

The government attempts to prevent the loss of semiconductor funds

Kevin Berghoff, CEO of Munich-based QuantumDiamonds, goes further: He argues the funding call was explicitly designed to protect the microelectronics budget after Intel slowed its Magdeburg plans. “The main idea of this €2 billion [approximately $2.3 billion] funding pot is to make sure the Intel subsidy is not used in other projects in non-semi fields,” he told EE Times in an exclusive interview. “The €2 billion that were in this funding call are the €2 billion that would have gone to Intel for the first phase of the project if they had started.”

With Germany’s budget under pressure, Parliament already reassigned €3 billion (approximately $3.4 billion) of what industry had assumed was a semiconductor-earmarked pool to generic infrastructure projects. Both Bösenberg and Berghoff describe a deep concern in Berlin and the Länder that remaining microelectronics funds—somewhere between €7 billion and €8 billion (Approximately $8.1 and $9.2 billion)—could be diluted unless they are attached quickly to credible industrial deployments.

That urgency shaped the unusually tight timeline: The call opened in mid-November 2024 and closed in early January 2025, Bösenberg said. “If you really want to make the call there in your ecosystem and you want a lot of applicants, then the timeline is too tight. But if you have a good overview of the projects that are likely to be submitted and your money is limited, then it’s enough time.”

Industry demand far exceeds supply. Companies requested roughly €6 billion (approximately $6.9 billion)—triple the available amount—according to Bloomberg reporting mentioned by Bösenberg. Some projects asked for enormous sums, including Ferroelectric Memory Company, which proposed a memory fab so large that it could consume nearly the full budget by itself.

Germany has issued what both executives describe as an early-start approval(in German: Vorzeitiger Maßnahmenbeginn), a legally precise instrument that allows companies to begin work “at their own risk.” No project has yet received its final Zuwendungsbescheid, the binding funding award.

“All expenditures that occurred before that official recognized project start are ineligible per se. But now, they have an official date,” Bösenberg explained. “Expenditures that occur from now on will get funded, but to what extent still needs to be defined.”

Berghoff echoed that view: “We can start the project without having the official go. We are still waiting for the German government to finally give us this formal document, which I think GlobalFoundries is also waiting for.”

This limbo reflects another bottleneck: Projects must be cleared by the European Commission’s competition authority before public money can be disbursed. German ministries have indicated support, but Brussels has not yet issued the final authorizations.

Projects are moving forward anyway

Despite the administrative caution, Germany has signaled political support in unmistakable ways, most visibly through GlobalFoundries’ high-profile event marking the formal launch of its site-expansion project under EU Chips Act rules. The event featured Germany’s top leadership: Federal Chancellor Friedrich Merz, Saxony Minister President Michael Kretschmer and GlobalFoundries CEO Tim Breen.

Bösenberg, who attended, called the appearance of the Chancellor a powerful symbolic gesture. “The hopes were that he would hand over the funding decision … but he didn’t,” he said. Still, the message was clear: GlobalFoundries’ expansion is central to Germany’s semiconductor ambitions.

The €1.1 billion (Approximately $1.2 billion) investment, confirmed in the company’s press release, focuses on scaling capacity at mature nodes, the workhorse technologies for automotive, industrial and communications markets. GlobalFoundries is adding front-end tools into buildings previously used for back-end operations, such as final test and packaging.

“It’s an upscaling of the activities they are already doing,” Bösenberg said. “Those are mature nodes, but of course it has to fulfill the first-of-a-kind requirements.”

The expansion also marks the first time GlobalFoundries Germany stated publicly that part of its output will serve defense applications—unusual in the German context. “This is new for Germans,” Bösenberg said.

While Saxony remains Germany’s semiconductor powerhouse, Bavaria is emerging as a beneficiary of the new funding model. QuantumDiamonds, an early-stage equipment company developing quantum-sensor-based metrology tools, received its own early-start approval.

QuantumDiamonds builds systems that use nitrogen-vacancy centers in diamond to image magnetic fields in 2D and 3D. The result is a non-destructive failure-analysis tool that can identify shorts, opens and layer-specific defects in complex advanced packaging structures, without the destructive procedures associated with X-ray, FIB or e-beam.

“The most exciting thing customers quote is that it’s non-destructive,” Berghoff said. “We image magnetic fields to tell you exactly which layer is affected.” Demand is coming from both advanced-node foundries and U.S. chip designers. QuantumDiamonds’ first two commercial tools ship in early 2026—one to Hsinchu, Taiwan, and the other to San Jose, Calif.

The company plans to construct a manufacturing facility costing between €100 and €200 million (approximately $116.2 and $232.5 million) expects non-dilutive public support covering up to 50% of the costs.

Funding geographies and what comes next

One unexpected outcome of Germany’s new structure is the way federal state cost sharing shapes competitiveness among regions. Under the current model, 80% of the subsidy comes from the federal government and 20% from the host state (Land).

That formula, Berghoff noted, gives Bavaria a structural advantage. “In Saxony, you have many, many projects, so the state has to make hard choices, whereas in Bavaria, it’s easier.”

Bösenberg agrees that competition among states (Länder) is now a defining feature of German industrial policy. Saxony, with Europe’s largest semiconductor cluster, faces heavier demand for its state budget. Bavaria and Baden-Württemberg, with larger and more diverse economies, can more easily co-finance first-of-a-kind experiments.

With projects receiving early-start approvals, Germany is poised to reshape its semiconductor landscape beyond the handful of headline-grabbing megafabs. The mix ranges from mature-node capacity and memory proposals to materials, chemicals and equipment—core segments where Europe has strategic weaknesses.

Still, the funding pipeline is likely to pause once these projects reach the formal-approval phase. “I do not expect a new call anytime soon,” Bösenberg said. “Maybe in the framework of the EU Chips Act 2.0.”

For now, the message from Berlin is that Germany intends to keep microelectronics in focus, even if Intel’s original plan was canceled. By accelerating diverse first-of-a-kind projects, Germany appears determined to prevent strategic funding from drifting elsewhere, while bolstering both established clusters like Dresden and rising ones like Munich.

See more:

Europe’s Chips Act Stumbles Over Its Own Rigidity

Dutch Clampdown on Nexperia Puts Europe’s Chip Sovereignty Strategy to the Test

data.title: The AI Future is Now All About the Edge
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

While we continue to see big investments in AI data center infrastructure and GPUs, the edge is where we are likely to see some of the real benefits and real value generation of this current AI boom.

Edge AI is not new as we have been writing about this for years. I recall headlines in EE Times like “Edge Intelligence Grabs the Spotlight at Embedded World” in 2019, and then in 2020, “Let’s Talk Edge Intelligence” and “Connected Edge Embedded Intelligence Requires a Smarter Edge.” What is different now is that the hardware is more powerful and the development tools are getting better, hence deploying models at the edge is becoming easier.

Additionally, we are seeing deployment of devices. For example, in a briefing last month, Ambiq CEO Fumihide Esaka said the company was seeing growth in demand for energy-efficient AI solutions at the edge. “We are very excited at the steady growth in adoption of edge AI,” Esaka told EE Times. “We are gaining new customers quarter-on-quarter, and in 2026 we expect healthy revenue growth.”

Esaka highlighted areas like robotics and wearable health devices as current areas of strong adoption, and he sees further opportunities in consumer wearables and industrial environments.

A recent report on AI at the edge highlighted how edge AI is not just a buzzword anymore—it will be the next battleground in silicon. “As billions of sensors and devices come online, they are generating an avalanche of data that can’t always wait for the cloud,” according to the report from corporate finance advisory and M&A firm Woodside Capital Partners (WCP). “Transmitting all that information to distant data centers is impractical due to latency, bandwidth, and privacy constraints. The solution? Push the computing—and the intelligence—out to the network’s edge.”

The report highlights what many commentators have suspected: that the big dollar investments in data center infrastructure will find it difficult to find a clear path to creating value that delivers a return on investment. However, edge is where that value creation can be more attainable.

“This new paradigm is ushering in ultra-efficient chips purpose-built for on-device learning and inference,” according to the WCP report. “These edge AI processors are no longer niche curiosities; they are becoming ‘the heartbeat of a new digital age,’ with the market expected to reach $13.5 billion in 2025. The strategic takeaway is clear: the future of AI will be decentralized, and the real innovation—and value creation—is happening at the edge.”

This was clearly emphasized at several events EE Times has attended over the last couple of months, such as at Silicon Labs’ Works With 2025 conference in Austin, Texas, the Synaptics Tech Day in San Jose, Calif., and Infineon Technologies’ OktoberTech 2025 at the Computer History Museum in Mountain View, Calif.

Silicon Labs: Growth in intelligent connected devices

At Silicon Labs’ Works With conference, Silicon Labs CEO Matt Johnson said AI would grow and move from the data center to the edge, and that represented an incredible market growth opportunity. Watch the video interview with Matt Johnson below:

The company also announced its Simplicity AI SDK framework, details of which were announced later in October and is covered here from the Bangalore, India, Works With event.

Synaptics: Open-source route to enabling edge AI

One of the common challenges we keep hearing about is the market fragmentation (and diversity of hardware and development tools) that restrict edge AI growth. So, the announcement from Synaptics at its Tech Day saw the launch of its Astra SL2610 edge AI processors. These are implemented using Google’s open-sourced Coral NPU IP (previously codenamed Kelvin). You can read the full story here.

I also moderated a panel at the conference entitled “Silicon, Software, and Scale: Making AI Work at the Edge,”in which executives from Google, Synaptics, Useful Sensors and Arcturus Networks explored why we are now at an inflection point for edge AI. They also covered some of the challenges in deploying LLMs at the edge. Full panel discussion video recording is available here.

EE Times also managed to sit down with Synaptics’ president and CEO on its strategy for edge AI growth. You can watch the video below:

Infineon Technologies: Delivering an edge AI development hub

At Infineon Technologies OktoberTech 2025, the focus was robotics, edge AI and power, as we reported previously here. One of the announcements from the event was the launch of Infineon’s Deepcraft AI suite making it easier for customers to integrate AI into their products based on Infineon’s PSOC edge microcontrollers. EE Times spoke to the company’s division president of connected secure systems, Thomas Rosteck, to discuss this and edge AI. You can watch the video interview below:

Removing friction in embedded system development for physical AI

A key focus for many companies has been the concept of physical AI—whether it is in robotics or other systems that can do some form of reasoning in the real world. In other words, primarily the edge.

As we mentioned earlier, that requires easier to use development tools to overcome the complexity of model deployment on multiple controllers and heterogenous architectures.  Hence, we saw Analog Devices (ADI) introduce CodeFusion 2.0, it’s open-source embedded development platform.

The latest CodeFusion Studio 2.0 now supports end-to-end AI workflows in one unified streamlined workflow, according to ADI. (Source: Analog Devices)

The latest CodeFusion Studio 2.0 now supports end-to-end AI workflows in one unified streamlined workflow, enabling developers to bring their own models and deploy them efficiently across the company’s processors and microcontrollers, from low-power edge devices to high-performance digital signal processors, according to ADI.

This new platform, based on Microsoft’s Visual Studio Code, features a built-in model compatibility checker, performance profiling tools and optimization capabilities designed to ensure robust deployment and accelerated time-to-market. A Zephyr-based modular framework enables runtime performance profiling for AI/ML workloads, offering layer-by-layer analysis and seamless integration with ADI’s heterogeneous platforms. This is intended to simplify machine learning deployment and enhance system-level performance insights.

“With this platform we are democratizing the building and deployment of models across a couple of dozen of the MCUs in our portfolio,” said Rob Oshana, senior VP of the software and digital platforms group at ADI, in a briefing with EE Times. “We’ve made everything open for extreme ease of use. Some of the unique features include a model compatibility analyzer.”

Opening up Armv9 based edge AI through Flexible Access

Finally, another move intended to open development of edge AI was the recent announcement from Arm that it is now offering its Armv9 edge AI platform to startups through its Flexible Access program. In a briefing with EE Times, Paul Williamson, who was at the time SVP and GM of the IoT business for Arm but has now moved on to a role in corporate ventures at the company, said that in the long term, the company was seeing a shift to the edge, with more and more customers looking at how to deploy edge AI. “People really are exploring use cases, for example SLMs [small language models] to do voice detection at the edge.”

The first Armv9 addition to Flexible Access that is available now is the Cortex-A320, which Williamson said was added very quickly given it was only launched in February, while the Ethos-U85 will follow in early 2026 (all the products available through Flexible Access are listed here).

Introduced in 2019 (see “Arm Introduces Flexible Access Program”), Arm Flexible Access gives startups up-front, low-cost or no-cost (for qualifying startups) access to a range of Arm technology, tools and resources. Startups need to have raised less than $20 million funding and have less than $1 million of annual revenue to qualify (see here).

Arm said the model has proven to be a catalyst for innovation, allowing companies to experiment and iterate designs freely, only paying license fees for the technology they use in final designs. Companies that have taken advantage of the program include Weeteq, Hailo and SiMa.ai. Arm said over the past five years, around 400 successful tape-outs have been achieved through Arm Flexible Access, with more than 300 active members participating. More than half of these designs target edge intelligence for embedded and IoT applications.

 “This program has been one of the big innovations for Arm,” Williamson told EE Times. “Historically, an Arm license would have been needed [by a startup], but now, startups don’t need to commit [to a license] until they are ready.”

data.title: Nvidia Invests $2 Billion in Synopsys, Targeting Broader Engineering Design Market
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Nvidia and Synopsys today announced what the companies call an “expanded strategic partnership to revolutionize design and engineering across industries.” The partnership involves Nvidia investing $2 billion in Synopsys, with both companies committing engineering resources to bring their combined design, simulation, verification and accelerated computing capabilities to any product development environment, not just chip design.

In the official announcement, the companies said that the expanded partnership will integrate the strengths of Nvidia’s AI and accelerated computing with Synopsys’ engineering solutions to deliver capabilities that will enable R&D teams to design, simulate and verify intelligent products with greater precision, speed and at lower cost.

As Jensen Huang, founder and CEO of Nvidia, and Sassine Ghazi, president and CEO of Synopsys, stood side by side to deliver the announcement via a webcast, Huang said, “Our [expanded] partnership will open new market opportunities for both companies. This is an exciting moment for both of our companies.”

 “What’s really exciting about this partnership is that it’s broad and deep between us and Synopsys, between using Cuda to accelerate the software, physical AI to emulate, and also connected into digital twins on Omniverse. It’s broad and it’s deep in scale, we have pretty significant teams assigned to each other to accelerate all of these software tools to create these new products that Synopsys can take to market,” Huang added. “The time is really now, and I think that the expansion of the market opportunity goes from Synopsys and the EDA industry addressing a several hundred billion dollars chip industry to now addressing a multi-trillion dollar ‘every product’ industry. In the future, every product will be designed in digital twins.”

In the media Q&A session, the CEOs were asked whether the investment would be used to buy more GPUs. Ghazi said that Synopsys was already a customer of Nvidia in the data center. “There is no intention and no commitment for that $2 billion to be used to purchase Nvidia GPUs. Hardware purchases happen in the normal course of business, as they have for many years.”

In response to a question from EE Times, Huang added, “I think it’s a great investment. We’re revolutionizing EDA, SDA, CAE, computer aided drug discovery, basically all aspects of R&D for anybody who does product design and product innovation and product manufacturing. And so, this is such a large expansion of the market opportunity. Along with the partnership, they’re [Synopsys] making such a large commitment on building on Nvidia. This was a wonderful way for us to show our commitment in the partnership.”

Ghazi chimed in, adding, “From a Synopsys point of view, the ‘why did we take the investment’ is really about optionality and acceleration. We can do the work we’re doing on our own. We’ve been doing it for seven years on our own. So, it’s not like we’re looking for motivation to do it. We know the market is going there.”

Ghazi essentially said that the next phase in the portfolio would be to look at how AI will change the engineering workflow itself, and how the tools can virtualize the entire system. “There are so many opportunities to truly change the way simulation is done, the various bottlenecks of design, how to accelerate it. So, for us it’s all about acceleration and grabbing the opportunity that we strongly believe is going to happen.” 

In prepared remarks for the announcement, Ghazi said, “The complexity and cost of developing next-generation intelligent systems demands engineering solutions with a deeper integration of electronics and physics, accelerated by AI capabilities and compute. No two companies are better positioned to deliver AI-powered, holistic system design solutions than Synopsys and Nvidia. Together we will re-engineer engineering and empower innovators everywhere to more efficiently realize their innovations.”

Partnership: the key initiatives

The expanded partnership will look at collaborations for the following initiatives:

  • Accelerate and optimize Synopsys’ broad portfolio of compute-intensive applications spanning chip design, physical verification, molecular simulations, electromagnetic analysis, optical simulation and more, using Nvidia CUDA-X libraries and AI physics technologies.
  • Advance agentic AI engineering to enable agentic AI workflows, integrating Synopsys AgentEngineer technology with the Nvidia agentic AI technology stack to enable autonomous design capabilities for EDA and simulation and analysis workflows.
  • Connect the physical and digital worlds through digital twins: enable the next generation of virtual design, testing and validation using highly accurate and sophisticated digital twins for industries like semiconductors, robotics, aerospace, automotive, energy, industrial, healthcare and beyond.
  • Cloud-ready solutions: making this accessible to engineering teams of all sizes by enabling cloud access for GPU-accelerated engineering solutions.
  • Develop joint go-to-market initiatives to broadly reach engineering teams across multiple industries with both on-premises and cloud-ready solutions, utilizing Synopsys’ global network of thousands of direct sellers and channel partners, building on Synopsys’ broad customer base and existing agreement to license, sell and support Omniverse technology embedded in Synopsys simulation solutions.

Accelerating engineering development and complete virtual testing

Ravi Subramanian, chief product management officer for Synopsys, told EE Times in a briefing that the partnership involved a tighter collaboration between the engineering teams of the two companies. “We want to accelerate engineering development and transform engineering workflows and bring in digital twins for different industry domains. There’s a clear commitment to achieving GPU acceleration for about 20 applications in 2026.”

 “The Ansys acquisition is seminal in the history of Synopsys. At Synopsys, we have about 2,000 customers, whereas Ansys brings a customer base of 18,000,” Subramanian added. “This opens up our solutions to many non-semiconductor customers. It opens up our market to anyone doing simulation-driven product design.”

This enables a huge new opportunity for Nvidia, as Tim Costa, GM for industrial and computational engineering and quantum, told EE Times. “Our route to market is through partners like Synopsys.”

Subramanian added, “This partnership accelerates the availability of accelerated computing [and simulation] to the wider product development community.”

Both companies also emphasized that the partnership is not exclusive, in that Nvidia and Synopsys continue to partner with the broader semiconductor and EDA ecosystem to create growth opportunities in engineering and design.

data.title: Chip Innovation for a Smarter Future: Grand Opening of IIC SHENZHEN 2025
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

[Shenzhen, 25 November 2025] — The International IC & Component Exhibition and Conference (IIC Shenzhen 2025), organized by AspenCore, a leading global media network in electronic technology, opened on Nov. 25, 2025, at the Sheraton Shenzhen Futian Hotel. The two-day event features an annual innovation product showcase, technical exchanges, high-level industry summits, and specialist forums, bringing together global expertise to explore new paths and possibilities for the electronics industry.

The opening day drew a strong turnout of industry professionals and generated a lively atmosphere, a testament to the semiconductor and electronics industries’ robust health and forward momentum. This year’s exhibition brought together leading companies and innovators from across the semiconductor value chain to present the newest advances and real-world applications in chip design, manufacturing, packaging and testing, and downstream uses.

The summit’s exhibition area displayed a wide range of innovations and products from well-known industry names such as Sigma Technology Group, Quiksol Global, Shenzhen HJC Electronics Co., Ltd, Shenzhen Kaixinda Technology Co., Ltd, Keysight Technologies (China) Co., Ltd., SmartSens Technology (Shanghai) Co., Ltd., Shenzhen Ruifan Microelectronics Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai YueYue Electronics Co., Ltd, Vadas International Co., Ltd., Biaoxin Technology Shenzhen Co., Ltd. , SHENZHEN GOCHEN TECHNOLOGY CO. LTD, Galaxy Semicon Group Limited, SEEDST INTERNATIONAL CO.,LIMITED, ICkey（Shanghai）Internet and Technology Co.,Ltd., Jiangsu  Runic  Technology  CO.,LTD, Shenzhen Sinox Electronics Co., Ltd, Shenzhen HDY Technology Co., Ltd., Hangzhou SDIC Microelectronics Inc., OK ELECTRONICA LIMITED, Shenzhen Iczoom Electronics Co., Ltd., MEET International Ltd., LINKCOST, Beijing Jingyuxing Technology Co., Ltd..

In addition to the product displays, High-level summits and a series of specialist forums formed the core of this year’s program.

With digital transformation and intelligent systems increasingly intertwined in 2025, the concept of “Digital Embodiment” — where AI interfaces with the physical world — has become a strategic priority in global technology competition.

In the opening address, Yorbe Zhang (Head of APAC/Head of Content, Aspencore) said: “Digital Embodiment is the key to achieving the fusion of ‘cognition + action’ in the AI era. It marks the shift from ‘cognitive intelligence’ to ‘action intelligence.’ Beyond redefining technological boundaries, it will reshape industrial structures, economic models and everyday life. Today, Digital Embodiment stands at an early stage characterized by ‘technological breakthroughs + scenario-based commercialization.’ Industrial manufacturing is the first sector seeing practical deployment, but broad adoption and the attainment of general intelligence still require solutions to core challenges such as perception, decision-making and energy cost. Global technological competition is moving from large models toward embodied intelligence; those who control the core technologies will shape the future industrial discourse. China has already included embodied intelligence in its policy planning, positioning it as an important pillar of next‑generation productive forces. As one of the world’s leading global technology media organizations, AspenCore has always been at the forefront of technological progress, and we hope this summit will deliver valuable insights to industry practitioners.”

The Global CEO Summit, centered on “Digital Embodiment,” brought together industry leaders and academic experts to examine frontier trends in AI, edge computing and smart hardware, and to advance collaborative innovation and ecosystem building. Speakers included: Dr. Wei Shaojun, President, IC Design Branch of CSIA, Professor of TsingHua University; Danny Perng, Senior Vice President, PacRim    Siemens EDA; Nitin Dahad, Executive Editor of EETimes; Yali Liang，EVP of Arm China; Rafa Jin，SmartSens SVP; Dr. Qi Wang, Founder and CEO of EnnoCAD; Philippe Notton, CEO and Founder of SiPearl; Leo Wang, Board Director, Chief Operating Officer, Executive Vice President of Worldwide Sales of VeriSilicon; Robert Lin, Chairman of the Board and General Manager of SigmaStar; Allen Wu, Chairman of CoreLab Technology; Angus Hsu, General Manager of Advantech (China) Embedded IoT Platform Business Group; Yalei Bi, Secretary-general of the Shenzhen Robot Association, and Head of the Shenzhen Advanced ICT Industry Cluster General Promotion Agency.

The summit’s roundtable, themed “Digital and Analog: New Paradigms in the Age of Intelligence”, hosted an in‑depth discussion with invited experts. Participants included Robert Lin, Chairman of the Board and General Manager of SigmaStar; Zhimin Tang, Dean and Chair Professor, Faculty of Computility Microelectronics, Shenzhen University of Advanced Technology; David Wang , Co-CEO of Chipsea Technologies (Shenzhen) Co., Ltd.; Alex Shang, Head of Sales Asia Pacific, Pragmatic Semiconductor ; Tony Yan, Vice President of Runic Technology; Shin Du,Senior Director of Sales, Imagination Technologies.

Throughout the summit, high‑profile global speakers convened to share the latest technology trends, outline key challenges, and propose response strategies, showcasing the boundless potential at the edge.

On the morning of the 25th, the conference hosted the 30th High Efficiency Power Management and Wide Bandgap Semiconductor Technology Application Forum. Technical experts from Texas Instruments, Innosilicon, Renesas Electronics, Shenzhen Kiwi Instruments Co., Ltd., AOS and others took the stage in succession.

That afternoon, the AI + Consumer Electronics Applications Forum followed. Speakers from VR Tuoluo, STMicroelectronics, Winbond Electronics, Keysight Technologies, SigmaStar Technology and Tencent Cloud presented in turn.

Multiple media partners streamed the summit live, enabling a hybrid on‑site and online audience around the world to experience the event simultaneously.

November 26 is the final day of IIC Shenzhen. In addition to the exhibition, the program will include the 2025 Global Distribution and Supply Chain Leaders Summit and the International Industry 4.0 Technology and Application Forum, among other events. In the evening, the 2025 Global Electronic Component Distributor Awards will be presented. Stay tuned for more highlights and join us for the closing day.

2025 World Electronics Achievement Awards Winners Announced

The World Electronics Achievement Awards were presented at the same evening. The Awards recognize companies and executives who have made outstanding contributions to driving innovation across the global electronics industry. Winning the Award is a high honor for both organizations and individuals. Nominees across categories—companies, leaders and products—represent the industry’s leading performers, reflecting their prominent status and exceptional achievements.

The winners below were selected by a judging panel composed of AspenCore’s senior global industry analysts, together with online voters from Asia, the Americas and Europe. The 2025 winners are as follows:

Company Awards & Editors’ Choice Awards

(winners in each category are listed in no particular order)

Innovative Product of The Year

AspenCore is a world-leading technical media organization in the field of electronic engineering. Its mission is to provide engineers, technicians, procurement and management professionals with the highest‑quality content to inspire creativity and drive market development across the electronics industry. AspenCore’s trusted media channels also offer an excellent platform for technology suppliers to reach technical decision‑makers.

[Shenzhen, 26 November 2025] On Nov. 26, 2025, the two-day International IC & Component Exhibition and Conference (IIC Shenzhen 2025) ended successfully. Bringing together outstanding exhibitors from around the world, the event presented a concentrated program of quality sessions that stimulated cross-border cooperation and innovation in the semiconductor sector. It highlighted frontier technologies and the industry’s robust expansion, attracting a substantial delegation of professionals from worldwide.

This year’s IIC convened many leaders and representatives across the global semiconductor value chain. Today’s Global Distribution and Supply Chain Leaders Summit drew particular attention. Distribution and supply chain experts from around the world converged to discuss “The Diverse Ecosystem Pattern of Component Supply Chains.” They explored the twin drivers of “Breaking Chains” and “Forming Networks,” dissecting how to overcome traditional supply chain limitations. By sharing insights on innovative cooperation models and on integrating emerging technologies such as IoT, big data, and artificial intelligence, the speakers highlighted pathways for achieving coordination across supply‑chain links and for building an open, collaborative, and intelligent global supply‑chain ecosystem that adds resilient growth momentum amid worldwide industrial shifts.

Mr. Jiguo Zhou, Chairman of Electronic Components Application and Supply Chain Branch of CIITA(ECAS), delivered the opening address. Featured speakers included: Alan Yan，CEO of Quiksol; Bertier Luyt, Co-founder & CEO of Adesio; Priyasha Sharma, Seedst International  Director; Ken Liu, Regional Sales General Manager of Heilind Asia Pacific; Grayson Li, Marketing Director of the Asia-Pacific region at Richardson RFPD; Echo Zhao, Chief Analyst of China, AspenCore; Dr. Moon-Kuen Mak, Managing Partner of Puli Consulting (Shenzhen) Co., Ltd.; Richard Lu, Sales Director of DWIN Technology; Jiang Lei, Founder & CEO of ICSuperman; Vitalii Savryha, Founder of ARDl Logistics, Co-Founder Ardi Express, Founder of Stealtho.
